Monorepo combining a Flask dashboard for visualizing air quality in France and DevOps documentation built with Quartz, featuring automated deployment to AWS (EKS) and Cloudflare Pages.

Dashboard DevOps AWS

Monorepo combining a Flask dashboard for visualizing air quality in France and DevOps documentation built with Quartz, featuring automated deployment to AWS (EKS) and Cloudflare Pages.

Overview

This project serves as a comprehensive monorepo managing both an application and its underlying DevOps documentation. The primary components are:

  • Air Quality Dashboard: A Python/Flask application providing data visualization for air pollution statistics across France.
  • Labs Docs: An internal documentation site built using Quartz to record and detail DevOps practices and labs.
  • Infrastructure: Shared Kubernetes (K8s) manifests to orchestrate deployments and define the infrastructure as code.

Architecture & Deployment

The monorepo architecture streamlines continuous integration and deployment using GitHub Actions:

  • The Air Quality Dashboard is containerized natively and deployed to a managed AWS Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster.
  • The Labs Documentation leverages standard static site generation and is pushed directly to Cloudflare Pages for rapid global edge delivery.
View on GitHub View Labs Documentation

Technical Details

  • Technologies: Flask, AWS EKS, Cloudflare Pages, TypeScript, Docker

An application for mirroring calendar events, built for iOS/macOS using Swift.

Calendar Mirror (MirrorCal)

A native Apple application (macOS menu bar and iOS app) that seamlessly synchronizes and mirrors events from multiple source calendars into a single unified destination calendar.

Application macOS

Application iOS

Introduction

MirrorCal is specifically designed to solve the problem of fragmented schedules. It perfectly combines professional, personal, and family calendars into one unified overview without accidentally mixing or merging the underlying accounts.

Key Features

  • Multi-Source Synchronization: Select and consolidate events from multiple platforms simultaneously (iCloud, Google, Exchange, Outlook, Local calendars).
  • Unified Destination: Choose a dedicated local destination calendar where all copied events will reside cleanly.
  • Smart Update Detection: Employs SHA256 hashing algorithms on event properties to meticulously detect changes. Only modified events trigger an overwrite.
  • Recurrence & Deletions: Gracefully supports recurring events and implements safeties algorithms for sweeping orphaned/deleted events.
  • 100% Local & Private: Operates entirely on-device using the native Apple EventKit framework. There are no external servers, no network calls, and absolutely no data collection.
  • Cross-Platform: Available as a lightweight, unobtrusive macOS menu bar utility and a fully localized iOS application.

How it Works

The user experience is built around a simple, transparent workflow running natively on the device:

  1. Grant Calendar access to the application gracefully via EventKit.
  2. Select one or more Source Calendars to pull event data from.
  3. Designate a Destination Calendar (creating an empty local calendar is standard practice).
  4. Define a Synchronization Period (e.g., from today up to +14 days).
  5. Initiate Sync Now. The app duplicates events accurately and automatically propagates future edits or deletions safely.
View on GitHub

Technical Details

  • Technologies: Swift, iOS, macOS, EventKit

Data mining & NLP projects in R featuring Bayesian classification, LDA/AFD, Word2Vec, TF-IDF, and advanced sentiment analysis with professional R Markdown reporting.

R Data Mining

Data mining & NLP projects in R featuring Bayesian classification, LDA/AFD, Word2Vec, TF-IDF, and advanced sentiment analysis with professional R Markdown reporting.

Overview

This repository contains a comprehensive data engineering and statistical analysis project focused on Bayesian classification, Discriminant Factor Analysis (AFD/LDA), and Natural Language Processing (NLP) using R.

Learning Objectives

  • Statistical Modeling: Implementing and comparing Bayesian classifiers and Discriminant Factor Analysis.
  • Natural Language Processing: Transforming raw text into actionable data using Word Embeddings (Word2Vec) and TF-IDF.
  • Data Quality & Engineering: Handling complex datasets, mitigating data leakage, and addressing paraphrased observations and noise.
  • Reporting & Visualization: Building professional, interactive HTML reports using R Markdown, custom CSS, and ggplot2.

Pipeline Features

  • Advanced NLP Pipeline: Robust text preprocessing (tokenization, cleaning URLs/mentions/hashtags, bigram detection) and feature engineering (transitioning from sparse TF-IDF vectors to dense Word2Vec representations).
  • Machine Learning & Analysis: Multi-class sentiment analysis of Twitter data, hierarchical classification routing through multiple model levels, and comparative studies analyzing the impact of different vectorization techniques.

Included Sub-Projects

  • Projet 1 (Bayesian Classification): Detecting emotions in short texts using Bayesian principles.
  • Projet 2 (Twitter Sentiment Analysis): Advanced study using Word2Vec and AFD to classify sentiments while managing data quality issues.
  • Projet 3 (Analyse des Thèses de Doctorat): Categorizing doctoral theses by comparing Sparse (TF-IDF) and Word Embeddings approaches.
  • Projet Final (Détection de Texte IA): Distinguishing human-written from AI-generated text for a Kaggle challenge.
View on GitHub View Interactive Reports

Technical Details

  • Technologies: R, Data Mining, NLP, R Markdown

End-to-end data pipeline to scrape, store, and visualize extensive animal data.

Scraping Animals Pipeline & Search Engine

A full-stack data engineering project designed to scrape animal profiles from A-Z Animals, store them in MongoDB, index them in Elasticsearch, and expose them via an interactive Streamlit Web App.

Overview & Collaboration

This repository represents an end-to-end data pipeline built entirely in pair-programming with an AI assistant. It demonstrates expertise in handling extraction logic, database management, search index creation, and interactive data visualization all within a containerized Docker architecture.

Core Features

  • Robust Scraper (Scrapy): Employs generic dynamic extraction for physical characteristics and "fun facts". Safely bypasses WAF/403 protections using scrapy-impersonate, feeding data directly into a MongoDB pipeline.
  • Search Engine (Elasticsearch): Enables full-text search with fault tolerance (fuzzy search). Includes an automatic fallback to a simple local search if Elasticsearch is temporarily unavailable.
  • Web App (Streamlit): Provides an interface to interactively explore the data with filters customized by diet, habitat, and country. Includes real-time statistics and Folium cartography.

System Architecture

The pipeline guarantees a seamless flow of data across multiple decoupled components:

  • Extraction: Scrapy extracts JSON payloads from A-Z Animals.
  • Storage: Data is persisted in MongoDB via PyMongo pipelines.
  • Indexing: Data is synchronized from Mongo to Elasticsearch for high-performance querying capabilities. Kibana is available for underlying index management.
  • Interaction: The Streamlit frontend acts as the user gateway, utilizing a custom SearchClient to interface securely with Elasticsearch and Mongo.

Deployment & Structure

The entire stack is containerized locally using Docker Compose allowing 1-click execution. The repository is clearly divided into specific modules:

  • scrapy/: Contains the Spiders, pipelines, and settings.
  • webapp/: The Streamlit application source.
  • scripts/: Utility scripts (e.g., triggering ES indexing).
  • tests/: A suite for unit and integration testing via pytest and flake8.
View on GitHub

Technical Details

  • Scraping: Python, Scrapy
  • Database & Search: MongoDB, Elasticsearch, Kibana
  • Frontend: Streamlit, Folium
  • Deployment: Docker Compose

An AI assistant leveraging Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to provide context-aware responses.

Time Groupe Assistant RAG

A virtual AI assistant exploration project leveraging Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) technology to provide context-aware responses based on a custom knowledge base.

Overview & Discovery

This repository serves as a practical discovery and implementation of modern RAG architectures. It was built specifically to act as a virtual assistant for Time Groupe, an event management company based in Montreal. By vectorizing their operational data, the assistant answers highly specific questions accurately without hallucinating.

Core Technologies

  • Backend API: Built with FastAPI and served via Uvicorn for high-performance, asynchronous request handling.
  • AI Orchestration: Utilizing LangChain to manage the prompts, LLM interactions, and data retrieval logic.
  • Vector Database: ChromaDB acts as the local vector store, caching the semantic embeddings of the knowledge base.
  • Large Language Model: OpenAI GPT-4o-mini handles the generative aspect of the responses based on retrieved context.

System Architecture

  • Knowledge Base: A raw text data file (time_groupe_info.txt) acts as the company's single source of truth.
  • RAG Engine: The rag_engine.py module is responsible for loading the text, chunking it into processable segments, storing it semantically in ChromaDB, and executing the similarity search retrieval.
  • API & Interface: The main.py exposes the orchestration via REST endpoints, while a lightweight static/index.html serves as the user-facing chat frontend.

API & Usage Context

Once the local server is running, the core interaction happens via the default POST endpoint (/ask). Users can submit natural language questions such as "Quels types d'événements organisez-vous ?" (What types of events do you organize?) and the system retrieves the top contexts from ChromaDB to synthesize an accurate response.

View on GitHub

Technical Details

  • Backend: Python, FastAPI, Uvicorn
  • AI & DB: LangChain, ChromaDB, OpenAI

A data engineering project enabling SQL analysis on NYC traffic accident data through normalization and a Streamlit dashboard.

NYC Accidents SQL Model & Analysis

A data engineering and analysis project focused on understanding database normalization, SQL creation, and data modeling using real-world NYC traffic accident data.

Overview

This project transforms raw, flat CSV data from NYC Open Data into a structured, relational SQLite database. It serves as a practical demonstration of moving data from an unoptimized state into a normalized architecture (targeting 3rd Normal Form).

Learning Objectives

  • Database Normalization: Structuring flat data efficiently to eliminate redundancy and ensure data integrity.
  • SQL Mastery: Writing complex SQL queries to extract meaningful insights using joins, aggregations, and advanced filtering.
  • Application Development: Bridging a robust SQL backend with a modern interactive frontend using Python and Streamlit.

Features & Architecture

  • Data Pipeline: Fetches live data via the NYC Open Data API, cleans the raw inputs, and automatically splits it into relational tables.
  • Interactive Dashboards: Visualizes accidents by borough, time of day, and severity using Plotly Express.
  • Geospatial Mapping: Plots specific accident coordinates on interactive maps.
  • SQL Playground: Provides a built-in interface to write and execute custom SQL queries directly against the new SQLite database.

Database Schema

The normalization process yields five core relational tables:

  • Lieu: Stores unique location properties (Latitude, Longitude, Borough, Street names).
  • Accident: Captures the core accident events linked functionally to Locations.
  • VehiculeType: A dedicated lookup table for various vehicle types (e.g., Sedan, SUV, Bike).
  • VehiculeInAccident: A join table tracking the specific vehicles involved in respective crashes.
  • FacteurContributif: Catalogs the variables contributing to the accidents, such as driver distraction.
View on GitHub

Application is Sleeping

To save resources, this server sleeps when idle. Waking it up may take about 1-2 minutes.

  • Data Pipeline: SQLite3, Pandas, Normalization (3NF)
  • Backend: Python 3.10+
  • Frontend: Streamlit
  • Visualization: Plotly Express
  • Source Code: GitHub Repository
